name: Scorpion
tagline: Armed with crushing pincers and a venomous tail, the scorpion has been one of nature's deadliest hunters for over 400 million years.
intro: From its sharp pincers to the venomous tip of its whiplike tail, a scorpion is well equipped for holding and stinging its prey. To insects, a scorpion is a deadly predator. A few kinds of scorpions can also be deadly to humans, but most scorpions are no more dangerous than bees.

facts.label: How it kills
facts.value: Kills by injecting venom through tail stinger

facts.label: Watch out
facts.value: Scorpions will lure their prey at night by glowing fluorescent pink and green! You can see this with a special "blacklight" flashlight.

facts.label: Where it lives
facts.value: Deserts, savannas, and tropical and subtropical regions

topics.title: Lethal injection
topics.text: All scorpions can inject venom. Although most stings are painful and would certainly kill an insect, they're not particularly dangerous to humans. The few types of scorpions whose venom has been known to kill people (mostly small children) live in the southwestern U.S., Mexico, North Africa, and Middle East.

captions.label: Ready, set, sting!
captions.text: When a scorpion contracts muscles at the end of its tail, this moves the stinger into position and forces venom into the hollow shaft.

topics.title: Special weapons
topics.text: Scorpions eat mostly spiders, insects, and other scorpions. They are well designed to deal with their prey.

captions.label: Clawed hands
captions.text: Scorpions have two special arms, called pedipalps. Instead of hands they have pincers, which they use to grab and hold food–and to hold a partner during the courtship dance.

captions.label: Scissor jaws
captions.text: A scorpion uses its scissorlike jaws–called chelicera–to cut up its prey. Next, the scorpion spits its salivary juices into the wounds, which partially digests the victim. Finally, it sucks the liquids through its small mouth.

topics.title: Midnight snack
topics.text: Scorpions can tolerate only a narrow range of temperatures. They live in warm places, but generally spend the daylight hours in the cool shadows. After the sun goes down, they come out to hunt, and any insect, spider, or other scorpion is fair game.

captions.label: Arachnid vs. arachnid
captions.text: Spiders such as this Mexican tarantula are common scorpion prey. Both tarantulas and scorpions are nighttime hunters, and both belong to the arachnida class of animals.

topics.title: May I have this dance?
topics.text: Before mating, male and female scorpions stand opposite each other, and the male grasps the female's pincers in his own. After walking around in this position for a while, the male deposits a packet of sperm onto the ground, which the female then takes into her body. After the female's eggs hatch, she'll carry the babies on her back until they're big enough to fend for themselves.

_provenance.note: Text liberated from original screen art; images & clip restored from disc. Original media is Microsoft/supplier copyright — placeholder pending swap to open-licensed assets.

video.title: Desert duel
video.caption: After sundown, the two opponents confront each other on the desert floor. They're both strong and quick. The tarantula has venomous fangs. The scorpion has pincers and sharp mouthparts, and a stinging tail filled with venom. On which contestant would you place your bet?
